purpose: Classify LinkedIn evidence about product-management workflows, pains, failure modes, ICP signals, JTBD, and buying triggers, then map only credible portions to documented Specsight capabilities.

product_truth.rule: All product claims and mappings must use capability and product-area identifiers defined in this file.

market_research: Product OS_Defn of done and Acceptance Criteria_part1.docx
Product OS_Defn of done and Acceptance Criteria_part2.docx
source_governance.external_assumptions: None added. Market interpretations are limited to the uploaded GTM research.

derivation_model.product_layer: Documented Specsight product areas and capabilities from specsight_product_taxonomy.json.
derivation_model.market_layer: Workflow, pain, failure-mode, ICP, JTBD, and trigger concepts synthesised from the uploaded GTM research.
derivation_model.mapping_layer: Explicit relevance and boundary rules connecting market concepts to documented product capabilities without adding new product claims.

discovery.name: Discovery
discovery.question: What customer problem or opportunity should be addressed?
discovery.fit_note: The product taxonomy does not establish customer discovery, strategy, prioritisation, or roadmap capabilities.

requirements_and_planning.name: Requirements and planning
requirements_and_planning.question: What behaviour is intended, and is there shared understanding before implementation?
requirements_and_planning.fit_note: Specsight can represent and enrich implemented product behaviour, but the product docs do not claim requirements management or automatic intent-versus-implementation comparison.

backlog_refinement.name: Backlog refinement
backlog_refinement.question: Is there enough shared understanding and confidence to implement responsibly?
backlog_refinement.fit_note: Current behaviour visibility, scenarios, flows, dependencies, and annotations may support refinement, but Specsight is not documented as a backlog-management tool.

sprint_execution.name: Sprint execution and continuous collaboration
sprint_execution.question: How is evolving implementation kept aligned with product intent while work is in progress?
sprint_execution.fit_note: Automatic syncing, scenarios, annotations, maps, and change history can improve visibility after relevant code changes are released to the watched branch; the docs do not claim live in-progress code review or requirement validation.

implementation_verification.name: Implementation verification and product acceptance
implementation_verification.question: Did the implementation exhibit the agreed customer and business behaviour?
implementation_verification.fit_note: Behaviour scenarios, flows, questions, change history, and exports can support review. The docs do not claim automated acceptance-criteria verification, test execution, or formal sign-off.

sprint_review.name: Sprint review and stakeholder learning
sprint_review.question: What changed, what was learned, and how should the backlog adapt?
sprint_review.fit_note: Current behaviour, flows, change history, Ask Specsight, and digests can reduce reconstruction of what changed and make stakeholder discussion more evidence-informed.

uat_and_release_readiness.name: UAT and release readiness
uat_and_release_readiness.question: Is there sufficient business, quality, and operational evidence to release?
uat_and_release_readiness.fit_note: Specsight can contribute behavioural context, history, scenarios, and portable artifacts, but the product docs do not claim test-result aggregation, readiness scoring, or release approval.

release_communication.name: Release communication
release_communication.question: How are meaningful product-behaviour changes communicated to stakeholders?
release_communication.fit_note: Changelog, digests, Ask Specsight, and exports directly support product-change visibility and distribution.

production_investigation.name: Production investigation
production_investigation.question: What changed in the product and which represented behaviours or feature relationships may be relevant to an issue?
production_investigation.fit_note: Specsight can supply behavioural change history, flows, relationships, and self-service explanations. It does not claim runtime observability, telemetry correlation, incident root-cause analysis, or customer-outcome measurement.

post_release_learning.name: Post-release learning and reprioritisation
post_release_learning.question: What did customers experience and what should change next?
post_release_learning.fit_note: The documented product does not connect production analytics or customer outcomes back to requirements or roadmap decisions.

onboarding_and_knowledge_transfer.name: Onboarding and product knowledge transfer
onboarding_and_knowledge_transfer.question: How can a new or adjacent stakeholder understand how the product works today without relying on tribal knowledge?
onboarding_and_knowledge_transfer.fit_note: Plain-language scenarios, feature maps, Ask Specsight, annotations, history, and exports directly support product understanding.

ambiguous_or_incomplete_acceptance_crite.name: Ambiguous or incomplete acceptance criteria
ambiguous_or_incomplete_acceptance_crite.description: Stories permit multiple reasonable interpretations, omit business rules or edge cases, or cannot be tested without assumptions.
market_source_topics: AI confidently builds the wrong interpretation
ambiguous_or_incomplete_acceptance_crite.boundary: Do not claim Specsight writes, approves, or objectively validates acceptance criteria.

shared_understanding_decays_during_imple.name: Shared understanding decays during implementation
shared_understanding_decays_during_imple.description: Customer intent is repeatedly translated across stories, code, demos, tests, and release artifacts, introducing assumptions and context loss.
signals: one more thing at the end
shared_understanding_decays_during_imple.boundary: Do not claim automatic comparison of intent and implementation; that is a GTM hypothesis not documented product functionality.

pm_reconstructs_what_engineering_built.name: PM reconstructs what engineering built
pm_reconstructs_what_engineering_built.description: Product depends on demos, Jira, pull requests, Slack, and engineering explanations to understand implemented behaviour and changes.
signals: engineering has to explain the feature
demo is the first time product sees it
market_source_topics: implementation knowledge trapped in code and demos
pm_reconstructs_what_engineering_built.boundary: Ground claims in represented product behaviour, not complete technical implementation details.

happy_path_demos_hide_edge_cases.name: Happy-path demos hide edge cases and nuances
happy_path_demos_hide_edge_cases.description: A demo shows the main UI path but does not reveal blocked actions, retries, permissions, validation rules, errors, or cross-feature implications.
signals: demo only showed the happy path
retry or cancellation was not covered
happy_path_demos_hide_edge_cases.boundary: Scenario visibility is not proof that the edge case was tested or works correctly at runtime.

subjective_or_late_product_acceptance.name: Subjective or late product acceptance
subjective_or_late_product_acceptance.description: Product reviews work only after engineering says it is done, without agreed scenarios or evidence, leading to rejection and rework.
subjective_or_late_product_acceptance.boundary: Do not classify Specsight as a formal acceptance workflow, approval system, or test-evidence repository.

fragmented_release_evidence.name: Fragmented release evidence
fragmented_release_evidence.description: PM must assemble QA status, engineering updates, acceptance criteria, known defects, support readiness, and release changes from many systems.
fragmented_release_evidence.boundary: Specsight does not document test status, operational readiness, feature flags, monitoring, rollback plans, or go/no-go approval.

release_changes_are_hard_to_communicate.name: Release changes are hard to communicate
release_changes_are_hard_to_communicate.description: Stakeholders need a concise explanation of added, changed, or removed product behaviour, but PMs manually rewrite technical activity into product language.
signals: engineering changes are hard to translate
release_changes_are_hard_to_communicate.boundary: Do not equate a Specsight digest with marketing release notes, legal notice, or customer enablement without human review.

product_documentation_drift.name: Product-behaviour documentation drifts from released code
product_documentation_drift.description: Separate product documentation becomes stale because releases continue while manual updates are forgotten or delayed.
signals: PRD no longer matches the product
release changed behaviour but documentation did not
product_documentation_drift.boundary: Use product-behaviour documentation, not all organisational or technical documentation.

feature_dependencies_and_blast_radius_ar.name: Feature dependencies and blast radius are unclear
feature_dependencies_and_blast_radius_ar.description: Teams review features as isolated tickets and miss connected journeys, dependencies, or downstream product areas affected by a change.
feature_dependencies_and_blast_radius_ar.boundary: Feature relationships are not guaranteed technical architecture, service dependency, data lineage, or impact analysis.

product_knowledge_is_tribal.name: Product knowledge is tribal
product_knowledge_is_tribal.description: Understanding how the product works is concentrated in experienced engineers, PMs, or long-tenured employees and transferred through meetings or shadowing.
signals: Slack is the source of truth
engineers answer the same questions repeatedly
product_knowledge_is_tribal.boundary: Answers are limited to context represented in Specsight and do not include undocumented strategy or external facts.

historical_product_context_is_lost.name: Historical product context is lost
historical_product_context_is_lost.description: Teams cannot reconstruct when product behaviour changed, whether a release affected it, or why a caveat or decision exists.
historical_product_context_is_lost.boundary: Do not claim complete engineering auditability or causal root-cause analysis.

production_issue_context_is_fragmented.name: Production issue context is fragmented
production_issue_context_is_fragmented.description: Product must correlate support reports, analytics, incidents, and releases to understand a customer problem, often rebuilding product context from scratch.
signals: customer issue cannot be mapped to a workflow
production_issue_context_is_fragmented.boundary: Specsight can provide product context but does not ingest telemetry, support tickets, or analytics according to the product docs.

validated_functionality_does_not_equal_c.name: Validated functionality does not equal customer value
validated_functionality_does_not_equal_c.description: Acceptance criteria and QA may pass while users behave differently, fail to adopt the feature, or do not receive the intended outcome.
signals: QA passed but users are unhappy
acceptance criteria met but feature is not useful
validated_functionality_does_not_equal_c.boundary: The product docs do not establish analytics, experimentation, adoption measurement, customer feedback synthesis, or outcome validation.

story_reopened_after_delivery.name: Story reopened after delivery
story_reopened_after_delivery.description: A delivered or tested story is rejected because expected behaviour was ambiguous, incomplete, or interpreted differently.
likely_root_causes: no agreed evidence or demo scenarios

dev_qa_ping_pong.name: Dev-QA ping pong
dev_qa_ping_pong.description: Work repeatedly moves between development and QA because expected behaviour admits multiple interpretations.

sprint_planning_becomes_live_discovery.name: Sprint planning becomes live discovery
sprint_planning_becomes_live_discovery.description: The team first discovers major requirements, dependencies, or business rules when it is expected to commit to the sprint.
observable_evidence: engineers see story for first time

day_three_blocker_or_spillover.name: Mid-sprint blocker or story spillover
day_three_blocker_or_spillover.description: Developers or QA become blocked after implementation starts because missing rules, dependencies, or edge cases surface late.

implementation_drift.name: Implementation drift
implementation_drift.description: Subtle deviations, undocumented assumptions, and technical compromises accumulate between intended and implemented behaviour.
likely_root_causes: implementation decisions not reflected back into product context

validation_drift.name: Validation drift
validation_drift.description: QA verifies the implemented function but misses original business intent or complete customer workflows.

reality_drift.name: Reality drift
reality_drift.description: Real customers and production environments expose behaviours or scenarios not represented in pre-release validation.
likely_root_causes: validation covers expected behaviour, not real-world outcomes

learning_drift.name: Learning drift
learning_drift.description: Production learnings remain scattered and are incompletely translated into the next iteration of requirements.
likely_root_causes: no persistent link across production observations, product intent, and future work

long_or_surprising_sprint_review.name: Long or surprising sprint review
long_or_surprising_sprint_review.description: Sprint Review becomes the first point where Product or stakeholders understand implementation details or discover important deviations.
likely_root_causes: reconstruction of changes during the meeting

meeting_based_release_decision.name: Meeting-based release decision
meeting_based_release_decision.description: A go/no-go decision depends on manually assembling and interpreting fragmented information during a meeting.

slow_product_onboarding.name: Slow product onboarding
slow_product_onboarding.description: New PMs or adjacent teams require months of shadowing, meetings, and repeated explanations to understand product behaviour.

engineering_interruption_load.name: Engineering interruption load
engineering_interruption_load.description: Engineers repeatedly translate code and product behaviour for Product, Support, Sales, QA, or Leadership.
observable_evidence: support escalates to engineering for product behaviour
likely_root_causes: product behaviour accessible only through code or experts

stale_product_artifacts.name: Stale product artifacts
stale_product_artifacts.description: PRDs, wikis, or behaviour documents no longer match the released product.

primary_champion.role: Senior Product Manager, Group Product Manager, or product lead
primary_champion.job_context: Responsible for customer outcomes across continuously changing product areas but unable to personally track every implementation detail.
primary_champion.core_jtbd: Maintain shared understanding of evolving product behaviour so I can make confident product decisions without repeatedly reconstructing context.

economic_buyer_or_sponsor.role: Head of Product, VP Product, or product leadership
economic_buyer_or_sponsor.job_context: Accountable for product-team leverage, cross-team alignment, delivery confidence, and decision quality as the organisation scales.
economic_buyer_or_sponsor.core_jtbd: Reduce the recurring context-reconstruction tax across product teams and make product behaviour easier to understand across the organisation.

important_collaborators.job_context: Contribute implementation, validation, coordination, or customer context and benefit from a shared behavioural view.
important_collaborators.note: The research positions Product as the champion; these roles are collaborators or secondary users rather than the default buyer.

ideal_customer_profile.company_profile: Product-led SaaS companies shipping continuously with multiple engineering teams or product squads.
strong_fit_characteristics: multiple engineering squads or cross-functional dependencies
growing customer base and support volume
Product Ops or another coordination function exists
product knowledge is distributed across code, Jira, Slack, demos, release notes, and people
observable_scaling_signals: PMs are becoming translators rather than strategists
Sprint Reviews or release meetings are getting longer
Support or Customer Success repeatedly asks Product what changed
Slack behaves like the product source of truth
weak_fit_characteristics: very small team where one or two people hold complete product context
no meaningful cost from cross-team knowledge fragmentation
primary need is discovery, prioritisation, analytics, experimentation, or project management

primary_jtbd.statement: When multiple teams continuously change the product, help me maintain a decision-ready understanding of how the product behaves today and how that behaviour changed, so I can make product decisions without repeatedly reconstructing context from engineers, demos, Jira, Slack, and release artifacts.
primary_jtbd.product_boundary: Decision-ready is a market framing. The documented product contributes current behavioural representation, self-service knowledge, relationships, history, summaries, and exports; it does not provide every input required for a product decision.

functional_jobs: Understand current product behaviour in plain language.
See normal paths, edge cases, errors, and connected feature flows.
Understand what represented behaviour changed across releases.
Ask product questions without routing every inquiry through engineering.
Preserve rationale, caveats, and links next to the relevant scenario.
Share product behaviour and change summaries with stakeholders.
emotional_jobs: Feel confident entering Sprint Review or a stakeholder discussion.
Reduce anxiety caused by incomplete understanding of implemented behaviour.
Avoid being surprised by product changes or hidden edge cases.
social_jobs: Provide credible, source-backed explanations to stakeholders.
Reduce the perception that Product is merely a translator between teams.
Help adjacent teams become more self-sufficient.

sprint_reviews_are_getting_longer.name: Sprint Reviews are getting longer or contain surprises
sprint_reviews_are_getting_longer.observable_signal: Product first reconstructs what changed during the review.

engineering_constantly_explains_features.name: Engineering constantly explains product behaviour
engineering_constantly_explains_features.observable_signal: Questions from Product, QA, Support, or Leadership repeatedly require an engineer to translate code into product language.

release_meetings_are_getting_longer.name: Release-readiness meetings are getting longer
release_meetings_are_getting_longer.observable_signal: Teams reconcile changes and evidence during the meeting rather than reviewing shared context beforehand.
release_meetings_are_getting_longer.boundary: Position Specsight as behavioural context, not a complete release-readiness or evidence-management system.

product_onboarding_takes_months.name: New PM or team onboarding takes months
product_onboarding_takes_months.observable_signal: Product behaviour is learned through shadowing, Slack, meetings, and long-tenured employees.

support_repeatedly_asks_what_changed.name: Support or Customer Success repeatedly asks what changed
support_repeatedly_asks_what_changed.observable_signal: Customer-facing teams lack a current, plain-language view of released product behaviour.

stale_docs_after_frequent_releases.name: Product documentation is stale after frequent releases
stale_docs_after_frequent_releases.observable_signal: The organisation no longer trusts manually maintained behaviour documentation.

customer_issue_investigations_are_slow.name: Customer issue investigations are slow
customer_issue_investigations_are_slow.observable_signal: Product repeatedly asks which behaviour changed, which feature areas connect, and which release is relevant.
customer_issue_investigations_are_slow.boundary: Do not position Specsight as production observability, telemetry correlation, or automated root-cause analysis.

multiple_squads_shipping_continuously.name: Multiple squads are shipping continuously
multiple_squads_shipping_continuously.observable_signal: No PM can personally maintain current knowledge of every feature, dependency, and behavioural change.

evidence_first_sequence: Extract explicit workflow stage, pain, failure, consequence, role, and trigger language from the post.
Classify market concepts using only controlled identifiers in this taxonomy.
Assess Specsight relevance independently from the existence of a PM pain point.
Map to product capabilities only when the post describes a need those documented capabilities credibly address.
Return none when the fit depends on undocumented functionality.

relevance_scale.direct: The post explicitly describes a problem closely matched by a documented Specsight capability, such as stale product-behaviour documentation, lack of plain-language product understanding, difficulty seeing feature flows, or difficulty knowing what behaviour changed.
relevance_scale.indirect: Specsight can materially support the workflow, but the post centres on a broader operating-model issue such as refinement, acceptance, release readiness, or production investigation.
relevance_scale.adjacent: The post provides useful market context, but Specsight addresses only one supporting information gap rather than the main problem.
relevance_scale.none: The post centres on capabilities not documented for Specsight, or the connection would require speculative product claims.

confidence_scale.high: The post contains explicit language matching the selected pain point, workflow stage, or failure mode.
confidence_scale.medium: The classification is a reasonable inference supported by multiple details.
confidence_scale.low: The mapping relies on sparse or ambiguous language and should be reviewed.

multi_label_policy.primary: Choose the single most central workflow stage, pain point, failure mode, and buying trigger when present.
multi_label_policy.secondary: Allow up to three secondary labels only when independently supported by the post.
multi_label_policy.product_mapping: Allow multiple product capabilities and product areas because one problem may span representation, knowledge access, history, relationships, or communication.

no_force_fit_rules: Do not map generic Agile, leadership, collaboration, velocity, or delivery advice to Specsight unless the post contains a specific product-context or behaviour-visibility gap.
Do not claim Specsight validates acceptance criteria, executes tests, proves correctness, approves releases, or measures customer outcomes.
Do not claim automatic comparison of requirements, intent, implementation, validation, and production unless such functionality is added to the authoritative product taxonomy later.
Do not classify general PRD writing, discovery, prioritisation, roadmap planning, analytics, experimentation, incident monitoring, or project tracking as direct Specsight fit.
When a post is about user value or adoption despite correct implementation, use specsight_relevance none unless it also contains a separate supported product-context problem.
classification_rules.quote_policy: Evidence quotes must be exact substrings from the LinkedIn post and should be short enough to support the chosen classification without reproducing the post.
classification_rules.missing_information_policy: Use null or empty arrays rather than inventing a buyer, trigger, failure mode, or product mapping.

no_fit_examples.topic: How to prioritise a roadmap using RICE
no_fit_examples.reason: Roadmap prioritisation is not a documented Specsight capability.

no_fit_examples.topic: A/B testing showed the feature did not improve retention
no_fit_examples.reason: Experimentation, analytics, adoption, and outcome validation are not documented Specsight capabilities.

no_fit_examples.topic: How to improve engineering velocity or estimation accuracy
no_fit_examples.reason: Generic delivery performance is not enough to establish a product-context or behaviour-visibility fit.

no_fit_examples.topic: Production latency incident and observability tooling
no_fit_examples.reason: Runtime monitoring and incident root-cause analysis are not documented Specsight capabilities.
